name: Fractional Reserve Banking
description: Fractional Reserve Banking (FRB) is the most common form of banking practiced globally. It involves banks holding only a fraction of their deposit liabilities in reserve, lending out the remainder. Proponents of mainstream economics argue that FRB is essential for economic growth, as it allows banks to create money through lending, increasing the money supply and stimulating investment. The system originated in ancient times, with goldsmiths issuing receipts for deposited gold and then lending out a portion of that gold, recognizing that not all depositors would demand their gold back simultaneously. Modern FRB is regulated by central banks that set reserve requirements, influencing the amount of money banks can create. Critics, particularly those adhering to Austrian economics and some conspiracy theorists, argue that FRB is inherently unstable, prone to creating unsustainable credit bubbles, and a form of legalized counterfeiting. They claim it allows banks to create money 'out of thin air,' devaluing existing currency and transferring wealth from savers to borrowers and the banking system. They point to historical bank runs and financial crises as evidence of the system's inherent flaws and argue it concentrates power in the hands of bankers and central banks. Mainstream economists counter that central bank regulations, deposit insurance, and lender-of-last-resort functions mitigate the risks associated with FRB and that the benefits of increased lending and economic growth outweigh the potential downsides. They also argue that money creation through lending is not the same as counterfeiting, as loans are backed by assets and represent a debt obligation.
subtitle: Money creation and economic leverage

claims.claim: FRB allows banks to create money 'out of thin air'.
claims.evidence: When a bank makes a loan, it creates a corresponding deposit, effectively increasing the money supply without tangible backing.
claims.criticism: Loans are backed by assets and debt obligations. The process is regulated by central banks, and money creation is not limitless.

claims.claim: FRB is inherently unstable and leads to boom-and-bust cycles.
claims.evidence: Excessive lending can fuel asset bubbles, and when these bubbles burst, widespread bank failures can occur.
claims.criticism: Central banks use monetary policy tools to manage credit growth and maintain economic stability. Deposit insurance protects depositors during bank runs.

claims.claim: FRB is a form of legalized counterfeiting that benefits banks at the expense of savers.
claims.evidence: Banks profit from lending money they don't actually have, diluting the value of existing currency.
claims.criticism: Banks provide a valuable service by facilitating lending and investment, which drives economic growth. Savers benefit from interest rates on deposits.

keyFigures.name: Murray Rothbard
keyFigures.role: Austrian economist and critic of FRB

keyFigures.name: Milton Friedman
keyFigures.role: Monetarist economist and advocate of FRB with regulation

mythologyConnections: The Midas Touch (creating wealth out of nothing)
Ancient concepts of debt and obligation

timeline.event: Goldsmiths start lending out deposited gold.
Emergence of modern banking systems with FRB.
Establishment of central banks to regulate FRB.
Global Financial Crisis highlights risks associated with FRB and complex financial instruments.

sources.author: Milton Friedman
sources.work: A Monetary History of the United States, 1867-1960
